title: The Lookout Shift
desc: Watch the UnderWorld from above. Log everything. Trust nothing.

steps.text:
Oipont's Lookout sits at the highest accessible point of Mount Pillar — a natural stone platform with sightlines across the surrounding territory. From here, you can see the approaches from the UnderWorld, the distant shimmer of Mipedian territory, and on clear days, the faintest suggestion of OverWorld highlands.

The outgoing lookout passes you the spyglass and the observation log. "Quiet shift. Two UnderWorld patrols at standard distance — they probed the eastern approach at dawn but pulled back. Normal posturing."

She pauses. "The unusual thing is the birds. Or rather, the lack of birds. The ridge usually has cave-hawks nesting. They cleared out yesterday. All of them. When the animals leave, something's coming."

options.label: Focus on the eastern approach — where the patrols were
Scan the ridge — find out what spooked the hawks

steps.text:
The eastern approach is a valley between two rock formations — the natural path from UnderWorld territory to Mount Pillar's lower entrances. You glass it systematically.

The morning patrol tracks are visible — bootprints in the dust, spaced evenly, the disciplined stride of UnderWorld soldiers. They came within two hundred yards of the perimeter, then stopped. The turn-back point is marked by a cluster of deeper prints — they stood there, watching, before retreating.

You log the distance. Two hundred yards is closer than last week's probe. The week before, they stopped at three hundred. The perimeter is being tested — not breached, but measured.

At midday, a new patrol appears. Three figures, moving along the ridge opposite your position. They're not approaching — they're observing. You can see the glint of a spyglass aimed at the lookout. They're watching you watch them.

You log it. Time, bearing, number, behavior. The patrol withdraws after an hour. The log now shows a pattern — daily probes, each slightly closer, each with longer observation periods.

The relief lookout reads your notes and nods grimly. "They're mapping our response times. Figuring out how fast we react to each approach distance." She picks up the spyglass. "I'll watch. You report to the gate captain. She needs to see this pattern."

options.label: Deliver the observation log to command

steps.text:
The ridge where the hawks nested is accessible via a climbing path — steep, exposed, not a standard patrol route. You secure your gear and climb.

The nesting sites are empty. Not abandoned — evacuated. The nests are intact, eggs still in some, but cold. The hawks left in a hurry, without the usual signs of predator displacement. No feathers from a fight. No claw marks. No broken eggs.

But there's a smell. Faint, chemical, rising from a crack in the ridge rock. You kneel and sniff. It's acrid — not sulfur, not mineral. Something organic. Something alive.

You peer into the crack. It's narrow — a fissure that extends deep into the ridge. The smell is stronger here, and you can feel warmth rising from below. Not geothermal warmth — this is humid, biological, like breath.

The crack runs for twenty feet along the ridge. You mark both ends. Whatever is below, it's large enough to produce heat and gas in quantities that spooked a colony of predatory birds.

You don't investigate further. You're a lookout, not an explorer. You log the anomaly — coordinates, smell description, crack dimensions, hawk evacuation — and climb back down.

The shift captain reads your log with an expression you've seen before: careful blankness. "We'll send a geology team. Probably just a new vent. The mountain develops them sometimes." She doesn't sound convinced. Neither are you.

The hawks don't come back.

options.label: File the report and watch the empty ridge
